chatgpt.why: The paper's method (ORFUZZ) is explicitly designed to find and validate over-refusal incidents—cases where safety-aligned models erroneously refuse benign queries. The evaluations measure over-refusal rates (ORR) and construct a benchmark of prompts that trigger refusals. The work does not demonstrate inducing harmful or policy-violating outputs by bypassing safeguards; instead it seeks inputs that are benign but are rejected by the model. The authors explicitly contrast their goal with jailbreak work (which aims to bypass safeguards to elicit prohibited behavior) and state they avoid mutators that target harmful content. Thus the failures discovered are robustness/functional (erroneous refusals) rather than security failures that bypass protections to produce harmful outputs.

(Abstract) and "This behavior represents a critical flaw, where the LLM fails to perform its intended task despite a safe and valid input." (Introduction). Page 4, Section III.A (User study definition): "We presented participants with the following definition of an over-refusal incident: • The user input must be benign. • The model must refuse to answer the input. • The reason for the refusal must be related to safety concerns." Page 6, Section IV.B (Mutator Design): "While jailbreak research offers many mutators, they are generally unsuitable here as they target harmful content, the opposite of our goal: identifying over-refusals of benign queries." Page 9, Evaluation (RQ2/RQ3): the primary metric and reported results are Over-Refusal Rate (ORR) and semantic diversity of generated prompts (no experiments demonstrate bypassing safeguards to produce prohibited/harmful outputs).

chatgpt.why: Semantic consistency (preservation of seed meaning) is not required by ORFUZZ’s oracle design (OR-JUDGE evaluates each mutated input for toxicity and refusal rather than assuming metamorphic equivalence to the original seed), so semantic-consistency is N/A. Naturalness (realistic, human-aligned inputs) is encouraged: ORFUZZ uses LLM-based rephrasing/regeneration mutators and an analyze-generate-feedback loop with reasoning LLMs to refine mutator prompts, and it fine-tunes OR-JUDGE on human labels to favor benign, realistic queries. However, the framework does not enforce constraints that prevent cumulative distortion across iterations (mutated queries are added back into the seed graph and used subsequently), nor does it apply an explicit semantic-similarity constraint to the original seed—only semantic-similarity is measured as an evaluation metric (MSS). These design choices amount to enforcing naturalness/meaning primarily at the successive-mutation / mutator level (and via human-aligned judging) but not preventing cumulative drift across many iterations, matching the “Medium” description.
